summary: Gruber argues that Apple's product development follows a distinctive pattern: launch a minimal, polished core product, then iteratively improve it year after year. He traces this pattern through Mac OS X, the iPod, and the iPhone, showing how each started with severe limitations but became transformative through steady, unglamorous iteration. He contends the iPad will follow the same trajectory, dismissing complaints about its initial limitations as missing the point. The piece frames Apple's engineers not as magicians but as artisans who get the fundamentals right and build from there.
key_points: Apple ruthlessly cuts features to ship a minimal core product, then builds outward through steady annual iteration
Mac OS X, iPod, and iPhone all followed the same pattern: underwhelming debut followed by years of compounding improvements
The iterative process is so gradual it's easy to dismiss in real time but obvious in retrospect
Apple's process doesn't just add features—it adapts, removing mistakes like the third-gen iPod's four-button layout
The iPad's initial limitations are not a weakness but a feature of Apple's deliberate platform-building strategy
money_quotes: They take something small, simple, and painstakingly well considered. They ruthlessly cut features to derive the absolute minimum core product they can start with.
It's a slow and steady process of continuous iterative improvement—so slow, in fact, that the process is easy to overlook if you're observing it in real time.
The designers and engineers at Apple aren't magicians; they're artisans. They achieve spectacular results one year at a time.
Rather than expanding the scope of a new product, hoping to impress, they pare it back, leaving a solid foundation upon which to build.
We may never see an iPhone that utterly blows away the prior year's, but we'll soon have one that utterly blows away the original iPhone.
The iPad really is The Big One: Apple's re-conception of personal computing.
So of course this iPad—the one which, a few years from now, we'll refer to off-handedly as the 'original iPad'—does less than we'd hoped. That's how the people at Apple work.
key_insight: Apple's genius lies not in revolutionary individual products but in a disciplined iterative process that starts with a minimal, well-considered foundation and compounds improvements over years.
